physics: poisson, linear_elasticity, helmholtz, hyperelasticity, dg_transport (Tier-2 fixture batch)
surface_symptom: Two distinct findings landed in the same iteration: (1) the deal.II 'cheap' Tier-2 bucket — pitfalls the falsifiability classifier rated as <80 lines of fixture code with no PDE solve — went from 4/10 to 10/10 with 6 new fixtures; (2) while running the new fixtures through verify_signal_clauses, the Tier-0 count reported by `verify_backend('dealii')` differed between the first call (93/96) and subsequent calls (96/96). The merge-gate tests at tests/test_signal_verification.py only saw the second-and-later answer because pytest/unittest re-runs setUp per test method, so the regression on test_tier0_floor_does_not_regress passed under discovery while FAILING when fired individually. This is a Tier-0 verifier whose result depended on call order — exactly the kind of silent test-quality failure the round-3 senior-AI-scientist critic warned about (Signal-clauses are an unfalsifiable contract unless every layer of the verification pipeline is itself deterministic).
root_cause: verify_signal_clauses.py:_load_entity_split() does `importlib.import_module('backends.dealii.element_catalog')` to pull in ELEMENT_NAMES (76 deal.II FE class names like FE_Q, FE_Nedelec, FE_DGQ). But sys.path was set up later, inside _harvest_pitfalls(), which is called AFTER _load_entity_split() in verify_backend(). So the very first call hit ImportError (silently swallowed) and built a 89-symbol set instead of 165. Every subsequent call — including the second test method's setUp — hit a warm sys.path and returned the full set. Result: a 7% drop in Tier-0 hit-rate (93/96 vs 96/96) that was invisible to CI. The 3 affected pitfalls were poisson#6 (link errors), linear_elasticity#6 (Lame ratio bias), hyperelasticity#4 (Cook membrane near-incompressible bias) — all relying on element-name tokens like FESystem that were missing from the 89-symbol set. Separately, the cheap-bucket probes also exposed catalog inaccuracies: dg_transport#5/6 Signals claim ExcMessage but deal.II 9.1.1 silently no-ops (n_constraints=0 / empty boundary_values map); the catalog text was correct because it explicitly mentioned both branches, but the falsifiability classification needed an exception_class → numerical_comparison correction. helmholtz#0 Signal said 'breakdown immediately' which deal.II's SolverCG never emits as text — empirically the observable is SolverControl::NoConvergence at max_steps when k^2 hits an eigenvalue of K.
pitfall_db_entries: [Integration] Verification harnesses that mutate global module state between calls are themselves untestable. If verify_backend('dealii') returns different results on calls 1 vs 2, no single setUp can establish the floor — discovery papers over the regression. Mitigation: ensure sys.path is set BEFORE any importlib.import_module() in the entity-loader path; add a test that runs verify_backend() twice in setUp and asserts identical Tier-0 counts.

[API] dg_transport pitfalls #5 and #6 in the deal.II catalog describe both an ExcMessage branch and a silent-noop branch — in 9.1.1 the silent branch is taken: DoFTools::make_hanging_node_constraints on FE_DGQ produces an empty AffineConstraints (n_constraints()==0) even on non-conforming triangulations, and VectorTools::interpolate_boundary_values returns an empty boundary_values map. Signal text was always correct; falsifiability classification was wrong (exception_class → numerical_comparison). Verify via numerical comparison.

[Numerical] deal.II SolverCG does NOT emit a literal 'breakdown' string. The observable for the helmholtz#0 indefinite-Helmholtz pitfall is SolverControl::NoConvergence raised after max_steps, specifically when k^2 is at or near an eigenvalue of K (operator singular). With k^2 well away from spectrum but still in the indefinite regime, CG can still converge serendipitously — so 'indefinite ⇒ breakdown' is too strong a Signal. Tier-2 fixture places k^2 at the m=4 eigenvalue of the 1D-FD Laplacian on n=32 nodes; SolverControl::NoConvergence fires reliably.

[Integration] Tier-2 fixture runner originally used sys.executable for python-mode fixtures unless backend was 'fenics' or 'dune' (which routed to dedicated conda envs). Kratos / NGSolve / scikit-fem all live in the repo's local .venv per task #18; running the runner with a different python (e.g. system python3) silently ModuleNotFoundError'd every python fixture. Fix: route backend ∈ {kratos, ngsolve, skfem} through `KRATOS_PYTHON`/`NGSOLVE_PYTHON`/`SKFEM_PYTHON` env vars defaulting to REPO_ROOT/.venv/bin/python.
agent_detection_after_fix: verify_backend() is now call-order-independent. The 6 new fixtures + the 4 already-shipped cheap fixtures + 1 medium-bucket fixture (forward_euler_instability) + 15 cross-cutting synthetic fixtures all pass under fresh invocation. tests/test_signal_verification.py floors raised: MIN_TIER2_PASSED 5→11, MIN_TIER2_RUNNER_PASSED 20→26, MIN_TIER2_PASSED_OF_CHEAP 4→10 (100% of bucket). Verify-script bug is fixed at the source and documented in an inline comment so the next person who touches _load_entity_split understands why src/ is inserted there rather than at module top. dg_transport / helmholtz pitfalls have falsifiability re-classified with empirical notes; Signal texts left intact (they already covered both branches). The post-execution critic can now grep for the actual exception text (`SolverControl::NoConvergence`) and the actual numerical observables (`n_constraints=0`, `boundary_values_size=0`) — not the misleading 'breakdown immediately' the original Signal threatened.
source_refs: scripts/run_tier2_fixtures.py — KRATOS_PYTHON / NGSOLVE_PYTHON / SKFEM_PYTHON env routing
task #51 — Validation audit: catalog text vs reality
task #53 — Close cheap deal.II Tier-2 bucket
